COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL
KNOWLEDGE BASE
RAG-Ready Reference for General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
Version 1.0 · General Health Informatics Edition · 2025
Structured for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) AI Systems
n DISCLAIMER: This document is intended for AI training and informational purposes only. It does not constitute
medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare professional for diagnosis and treatment decisions. For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification. Page 1
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
1. VITAL SIGNS — REFERENCE RANGES &
INTERPRETATION
1.1 Blood Pressure (BP)
Blood pressure is the force exerted by circulating blood on arterial walls, measured in mmHg as
systolic/diastolic. It is the primary indicator of cardiovascular health. Category Systolic (mmHg) Diastolic (mmHg) Clinical Action
Normal < 120 < 80 No intervention; maintain lifestyle. Elevated 120–129 < 80 Lifestyle changes; monitor regularly. Hypertension Stage 1 130–139 80–89 Lifestyle + consider medication. Hypertension Stage 2 ‡ 140 ‡ 90 Medication + lifestyle modification. Hypertensive Crisis > 180 > 120 Seek emergency care immediately. Hypotension < 90 < 60 Evaluate cause; fluids/medication. 1.2 Heart Rate (Pulse)
Status Range Notes
Bradycardia < 60 bpm Normal in athletes; pathological if symptomatic. Normal Adult 60–100 bpm Resting heart rate for healthy adults. Tachycardia > 100 bpm Fever, anemia, anxiety, cardiac arrhythmia. Critical High > 150 bpm Immediate evaluation required. 1.3 Respiratory Rate
Status Range Cause/Notes
Normal Adult 12–20 breaths/min Eupnea — normal breathing. Tachypnoea > 20 breaths/min Fever, anxiety, pulmonary disease, acidosis. Bradypnoea < 12 breaths/min Opioid overdose, CNS depression. Apnoea 0 Cessation — emergency. 1.4 Body Temperature
Status Temperature Clinical Notes
Hypothermia < 35 °C / < 95 °F Shivering, confusion, cardiac risk. Normal 36.1–37.2 °C / 97–99 °F Oral measurement. Low-grade fever 37.3–38.0 °C / 99.1–100.4°F Monitor; hydrate. Fever 38.1–39.9 °C / 100.5–103.8°F Antipyretics; investigate cause. High Fever 40–41 °C / 104–105.8 °F Urgent medical attention. For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification. Page 2
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
Status Temperature Clinical Notes
Hyperpyrexia > 41.1 °C / > 106 °F Emergency — risk of organ damage. 1.5 Oxygen Saturation (SpOn)
Status SpOn Range Action
Normal 95–100% Healthy perfusion. Acceptable (COPD) 88–94% Target for COPD patients. Mild Hypoxemia 91–94% Supplemental On may be needed. Moderate Hypoxemia 86–90% Supplemental On required. Severe Hypoxemia < 86% Emergency oxygen therapy. 1.6 Body Mass Index (BMI)
Category BMI (kg/m²) Recommendation
Underweight < 18.5 Nutritional assessment; risk of deficiency. Normal weight 18.5–24.9 Healthy range. Overweight 25–29.9 Lifestyle modification advised. Obese Class I 30–34.9 Medical evaluation; diet & exercise plan. Obese Class II 35–39.9 High risk; consider specialist referral. Obese Class III ‡ 40 Very high risk; bariatric evaluation. For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification.

Page 3
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
2. COMPLETE BLOOD COUNT (CBC) — FULL
REFERENCE
The CBC is one of the most common lab tests. It measures cellular components of blood and is used to detect
infection, anemia, clotting problems, immune disorders, and more. 2.1 Red Blood Cell (RBC) Parameters
Parameter Male Ref. Female Ref. Significance
RBC Count 4.5–5.9 M/µL 4.0–5.2 M/µL fl anemia; › polycythemia
Hemoglobin (Hgb) 13.5–17.5 g/dL 12.0–15.5 g/dL Oxygen-carrying protein
Hematocrit (Hct) 41–53% 36–46% % blood volume = RBCs
MCV 80–100 fL 80–100 fL Size of RBC
MCH 27–33 pg 27–33 pg Hgb per RBC
MCHC 32–36 g/dL 32–36 g/dL Hgb concentration in RBC
RDW 11.5–14.5% 11.5–14.5% RBC size variation
2.2 White Blood Cell (WBC) Differential
Cell Type Normal Range Function/Significance
Total WBC 4,500–11,000 /µL Immune defense total count
Neutrophils 50–70% / 1800–7800 Bacterial infection fighters
Lymphocytes 20–40% / 1000–4800 Viral immunity; B & T cells
Monocytes 2–8% / 200–950 Macrophage precursors
Eosinophils 1–4% / 15–500 Allergy & parasitic response
Basophils 0.5–1% / 0–100 Inflammatory mediators
Bands (immature) 3–5% › in serious infection (left shift)
2.3 WBC Abnormalities Interpretation
Condition Threshold Common Causes
Neutrophilia > 7,800/µL Bacterial infection, inflammation, stress, steroids
Neutropenia < 1,800/µL Viral illness, chemotherapy, aplastic anemia
Lymphocytosis > 4,800/µL Viral infections (EBV, CMV), CLL
Lymphopenia < 1,000/µL HIV, SLE, corticosteroids, radiation
Eosinophilia > 500/µL Allergy, asthma, parasitic infection
Leukocytosis > 11,000/µL Infection, leukemia, inflammation
Leukopenia < 4,500/µL Bone marrow failure, autoimmune, viral
For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification. Page 4
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
2.4 Platelets
Status Range Clinical Notes
Normal 150,000–400,000/µL Adequate clotting capacity. Thrombocytopenia < 150,000/µL Bleeding risk; flfl if < 50,000. Mild Thrombo. 100,000–150,000/µL Monitor; investigate cause. Severe Thrombo. < 20,000/µL Spontaneous bleeding risk; treat urgently. Thrombocytosis > 400,000/µL Reactive (infection) or essential (MPD). For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification. Page 5
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
3.

METABOLIC PANEL & CHEMISTRY
3.1 Basic Metabolic Panel (BMP)
Test Reference Range Interpretation
Glucose (Fasting) 70–99 mg/dL < 70 hypoglycemia; ‡ 126 diabetes
Glucose (Post-meal) < 140 mg/dL (2hr) 140–199 pre-diabetes; ‡ 200 diabetes
BUN (Blood Urea N) 7–25 mg/dL › renal disease, dehydration
Creatinine 0.6–1.2 mg/dL (M) 0.5–1.1 (F); › = renal dysfunction
eGFR > 60 mL/min/1.73m² < 60 for > 3mo = CKD
Sodium (Na+) 136–145 mEq/L Fluid/osmolality balance
Potassium (K+) 3.5–5.0 mEq/L Critical: < 3.0 or > 6.0 = emergency
Chloride (Cl-) 98–107 mEq/L Acid-base balance
COn (Bicarb) 22–29 mEq/L fl = metabolic acidosis; › = alkalosis
Calcium (Ca) 8.5–10.5 mg/dL Bone, nerve, muscle function
Anion Gap 8–16 mEq/L › = MUDPILES causes of acidosis
3.2 Liver Function Tests (LFTs)
Test Reference Range Clinical Significance
ALT (SGPT) 7–56 U/L Liver-specific; ›› = hepatitis/liver injury
AST (SGOT) 10–40 U/L Liver + heart/muscle; less specific
ALP 44–147 U/L › liver/bone disease; ›› cholestasis
GGT 8–61 U/L Alcohol use, liver disease, medications
Bilirubin (Total) 0.1–1.2 mg/dL › = jaundice, hemolysis, liver disease
Bilirubin (Direct) 0–0.3 mg/dL › = obstructive jaundice
Albumin 3.5–5.0 g/dL Liver synthesis; fl = malnutrition/cirrhosis
Total Protein 6.3–8.2 g/dL Nutritional status, liver/kidney disease
PT/INR INR 0.8–1.1 Clotting; › = liver disease, warfarin
3.3 Kidney Function Panel
Test Reference Range Notes
Creatinine 0.6–1.2 mg/dL Best single marker of GFR
BUN/Creatinine Ratio 10:1–20:1 › = dehydration/GI bleed; fl = malnutrition
Uric Acid 3.4–7.0 mg/dL (M) › = gout, renal failure, leukemia
Phosphorus 2.5–4.5 mg/dL Regulated by kidney; › in CKD
Magnesium 1.7–2.2 mg/dL fl = diarrhea, alcoholism, poor diet
For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification. Page 6
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
Test Reference Range Notes
Cystatin C 0.62–1.15 mg/L More accurate GFR marker than creatinine
3.4 Lipid Panel
Parameter Desirable Range Interpretation
Total Cholesterol < 200 mg/dL Desirable; 200–239 borderline; ‡240 high
LDL Cholesterol < 100 mg/dL Optimal; < 70 for very high CVD risk
HDL Cholesterol > 60 mg/dL < 40 (M) / < 50 (F) = risk factor
Triglycerides < 150 mg/dL 150–199 borderline; ‡ 200 high
Non-HDL Chol. < 130 mg/dL Total Chol - HDL; atherogenic particles
LDL/HDL Ratio < 3.5 › ratio = › cardiovascular risk
For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification. Page 7
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
4. DIABETES & ENDOCRINE PANEL
4.1 Diabetes Diagnostic Criteria (ADA 2024)
Status HbA1c Fasting Glucose 2-hr OGTT Action
Normal < 5.7% < 100 mg/dL < 140 mg/dL No action needed. Pre-Diabetes 5.7–6.4% 100–125 140–199 Lifestyle intervention. Diabetes ‡ 6.5% ‡ 126 ‡ 200 Diagnose & manage. Diabetes
N/A N/A ‡ 200 + sx Random plasma glucose.

(symptom)
4.2 HbA1c to Estimated Average Glucose (eAG)
HbA1c Estimated Average Glucose
5% 97 mg/dL (5.4 mmol/L)
6% 126 mg/dL (7.0)
7% 154 mg/dL (8.6)
8% 183 mg/dL (10.2)
9% 212 mg/dL (11.8)
10% 240 mg/dL (13.4)
11% 269 mg/dL (14.9)
12% 298 mg/dL (16.5)
4.3 Thyroid Function Tests
Test Reference Range Clinical Significance
TSH 0.4–4.0 mIU/L Primary thyroid screening test
Free T4 0.8–1.8 ng/dL Active hormone; fl hypothyroid; › hyperthyroid
Free T3 2.3–4.2 pg/mL Most active form; cellular metabolism
Total T4 5.0–12.0 µg/dL Includes bound + free T4
Anti-TPO < 35 IU/mL › = Hashimoto's or Graves' disease
Anti-Tg < 20 IU/mL Thyroid autoimmunity marker
Condition TSH Free T4 Free T3 Features
Hypothyroidism (Primary) › TSH fl T4 Normal/fl T3 Fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance
Hyperthyroidism (Primary) fl TSH › T4 › T3 Anxiety, weight loss, tachycardia
Subclinical Hypothyroidism › TSH Normal Normal Monitor; may treat if symptomatic
Subclinical Hyperthyroidism fl TSH Normal Normal AF risk; bone loss risk
Secondary Hypothyroidism fl TSH fl T4 fl T3 Pituitary/hypothalamic disease
For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification. Page 8
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
5. CARDIAC BIOMARKERS & COAGULATION
5.1 Cardiac Biomarkers
Biomarker Reference Range Clinical Significance
Troponin I (cTnI) < 0.04 ng/mL Rises 3–6 hr post MI; peaks 12–24 hr; falls 7–10d
Troponin T (cTnT) < 0.01 ng/mL Similar to TnI; also renal disease
hs-Troponin < 14 ng/L High-sensitivity; detects minor myocardial injury
CK-MB < 6.3 ng/mL (M) Cardiac isoform; rises 4–6hr, falls by 48h
BNP < 100 pg/mL Heart failure marker; › with cardiac stress
NT-proBNP < 300 pg/mL (rule out) Better stability than BNP
Myoglobin < 90 ng/mL Earliest MI marker (1–3hr); non-specific
LDH 140–280 U/L General tissue damage; multiple sources
CK (Total) 38–174 U/L (M) Muscle damage (heart, skeletal, brain)
5.2 Coagulation Studies
Test Reference Range Notes
PT 11–13.5 seconds Extrinsic pathway; warfarin monitoring
INR 0.8–1.1 (normal) 2–3 therapeutic for AF; 2.5–3.5 for prosthetic valves
aPTT 25–35 seconds Intrinsic pathway; heparin monitoring
Fibrinogen 200–400 mg/dL fl = DIC, liver disease; › = inflammation
D-Dimer < 0.5 µg/mL FEU › = DVT/PE; elevated in COVID-19, pregnancy
Thrombin Time 15–19 seconds Final clotting step; › = heparin, fibrinogen issue
Platelet Function Varies by test Closure time (CT): Collagen/ADP < 62–100 sec
For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification. Page 9
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
6. URINALYSIS & URINE CHEMISTRY
Urinalysis (UA) is a panel of tests on urine used to detect urinary tract infections, kidney disease, diabetes, and
other systemic disorders.

6.1 Routine Urinalysis Parameters
Parameter Normal Interpretation
Color Pale yellow to amber Dark = dehydration; red = hematuria; orange = bilirubinuria
Clarity Clear Cloudy = UTI, crystals, lipids
Specific Gravity 1.005–1.030 < 1.005 = dilute (DI, overhydration); > 1.030 = dehydration
pH 4.5–8.0 < 5 = acidic (diabetes, dehydration); > 7 = UTI, vegetarian
Protein Negative (< 8 mg/dL) › = nephropathy, UTI, pre-eclampsia
Glucose Negative › = diabetes (blood glucose > 180 mg/dL)
Ketones Negative › = DKA, fasting, low-carb diet
Blood/Hemoglobin Negative › = hematuria, hemolysis, myoglobinuria
Leukocyte Esterase Negative › = UTI, pyuria
Nitrites Negative › = gram-negative bacteria (E. coli)
Bilirubin Negative › = liver disease, obstructive jaundice
Urobilinogen 0.2–1.0 EU/dL › = hemolysis, liver disease; fl = biliary obstruction
6.2 Urine Microscopy
Finding Normal Significance
RBCs 0–2 /HPF › = UTI, glomerulonephritis, stones, malignancy
WBCs 0–5 /HPF › = UTI, pyelonephritis, interstitial nephritis
Epithelial Cells Few Squamous = contamination; renal tubular = tubular damage
Hyaline Casts 0–2 /LPF Normal; › after exercise or fever
RBC Casts None Glomerulonephritis — always pathological
WBC Casts None Pyelonephritis, interstitial nephritis
Granular Casts None Acute tubular necrosis, advanced kidney disease
Bacteria None › = UTI; quantify with culture (> 100,000 CFU/mL)
Crystals Few (varies) Oxalate/urate: stones risk; cystine: cystinuria
6.3 Urine Protein Quantification
Test Normal Range Clinical Significance
Spot Urine Protein/Creatinine < 0.2 mg/mg Normal; > 3.5 = nephrotic range
150–3499 = proteinuria; ‡ 3500 =
24-hr Urine Protein < 150 mg/day
nephrotic
For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification. Page 10
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
Test Normal Range Clinical Significance
Microalbuminuria 30–300 mg/day Early diabetic/hypertensive nephropathy
Macroalbuminuria > 300 mg/day Significant kidney disease
For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification. Page 11
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
7.

IMMUNOLOGY, INFLAMMATION & SPECIAL MARKERS
7.1 Inflammatory Markers
Marker Normal Interpretation
CRP (C-Reactive Protein) < 1.0 mg/dL (std) 1–3 = moderate; > 3 = high CVD risk; > 10 = infection
hs-CRP < 1.0 mg/L < 1 = low; 1–3 = moderate; > 3 = high CV risk
ESR (Erythrocyte Sed Rate) M: < 15; F: < 20 mm/hr › = infection, autoimmune, malignancy, anemia
Procalcitonin (PCT) < 0.1 ng/mL › = bacterial sepsis; guides antibiotic use
› = inflammation, iron overload, malignancy; fl = iron
Ferritin M: 30–400; F: 13–150 ng/mL
deficiency
IL-6 < 7 pg/mL Cytokine storm marker; elevated in COVID-19
7.2 Autoimmune Panel
Test Normal Value Clinical Significance
ANA (Antinuclear Ab) Negative (< 1:40) › = SLE, Sjögren's, scleroderma
Anti-dsDNA < 30 IU/mL Specific for SLE; correlates with disease activity
Anti-Smith Negative Highly specific for SLE
RF (Rheumatoid Factor) < 14 IU/mL › = RA, Sjögren's; non-specific
Anti-CCP < 20 U/mL Highly specific for RA; early detection
ANCA (c-ANCA/p-ANCA) Negative Vasculitis; c-ANCA: GPA; p-ANCA: MPA
Complement C3 90–180 mg/dL fl = SLE, glomerulonephritis
Complement C4 16–47 mg/dL fl = SLE, hereditary angioedema
Anti-Ro/SS-A Negative Sjögren's, neonatal lupus
Anti-La/SS-B Negative Sjögren's syndrome
7.3 Vitamins & Minerals Panel
Nutrient Reference Range Clinical Significance
Vitamin D (25-OH) 30–100 ng/mL < 20 = deficient; 20–29 = insufficient
Vitamin B12 200–900 pg/mL < 200 = deficiency (neuro/anemia risk)
Folate (Serum) 2.7–17.0 ng/mL < 2.7 = deficiency; megaloblastic anemia risk
Iron (Serum) 60–170 µg/dL fl = iron deficiency; › = hemochromatosis
TIBC 250–370 µg/dL › in iron deficiency; fl in chronic disease
Transferrin Sat. 20–50% < 16% = iron deficiency; > 50% = iron overload
Zinc 70–120 µg/dL fl = immune deficiency, poor wound healing
Copper 70–140 µg/dL › Wilson's disease; fl Menkes disease
For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification. Page 12
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
Nutrient Reference Range Clinical Significance
Magnesium 1.7–2.2 mg/dL Cardiac arrhythmia if < 1.0 mg/dL
Vitamin A (Retinol) 30–65 µg/dL fl = night blindness, immune deficiency
Vitamin E 5.5–17 µg/mL Antioxidant; fl = hemolytic anemia
For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification. Page 13
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
8.

HORMONAL PANEL — REPRODUCTIVE & ADRENAL
8.1 Female Reproductive Hormones
Hormone Reference Range Clinical Significance
FSH 3.5–12.5 IU/L (follicular) › = menopause, premature ovarian insufficiency
LH 2.4–12.6 IU/L (follicular) › surge = ovulation; › baseline = PCOS/menopause
Estradiol 20–150 pg/mL (follicular) fl = menopause, hypoestrogenism
Progesterone 5–20 ng/mL (mid-luteal) fl = anovulation, luteal phase defect
Prolactin < 25 ng/mL › = pituitary adenoma, medications, hypothyroidism
AMH 1.5–4.0 ng/mL Ovarian reserve; fl = diminished reserve
Beta-hCG < 5 mIU/mL (non-pregnant) Doubles every 48h in early normal pregnancy
DHEA-S 65–380 µg/dL Adrenal androgen; › = PCOS, CAH, adrenal tumor
8.2 Male Reproductive Hormones
Hormone Reference Range Clinical Significance
Total Testosterone 300–1000 ng/dL fl = hypogonadism, obesity, chronic illness
Free Testosterone 8.7–25.1 pg/mL More clinically relevant than total
SHBG 10–57 nmol/L › = hyperthyroidism, liver disease; fl = obesity
PSA < 4.0 ng/mL › = prostate cancer, BPH, prostatitis
FSH (Male) 1.5–12.4 IU/L › = primary testicular failure
LH (Male) 1.7–8.6 IU/L fl = secondary hypogonadism
Inhibin B > 80 pg/mL Sertoli cell function; spermatogenesis marker
8.3 Adrenal & Stress Hormones
Hormone Reference Range Clinical Significance
Cortisol (AM 8am) 10–20 µg/dL Peak morning; › = Cushing's; fl = Addison's
Cortisol (PM 4pm) 3–10 µg/dL Diurnal variation; loss = Cushing's
ACTH 10–60 pg/mL (8am) › = Addison's, Cushing's disease; fl = cortisol-secreting tumor
Aldosterone 2.7–27.8 ng/dL (upright) › = primary aldosteronism (Conn's)
Renin 0.6–4.3 ng/mL/hr › = renovascular hypertension; fl = primary aldosteronism
Epinephrine < 100 pg/mL › = pheochromocytoma, stress
Norepinephrine 70–750 pg/mL › = pheochromocytoma, neuroblastoma
For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification. Page 14
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis

CKD Stage eGFR (mL/min/1.73m²) Description Management

damage

10. TUMOUR MARKERS & ONCOLOGY PANEL
Tumour markers are substances produced by cancer cells or normal cells in response to cancer. They aid
diagnosis, monitor treatment response, and detect recurrence. No marker alone confirms or rules out cancer.

Tumour Marker Normal Value Associated Cancer Notes
BPH, prostatitis also elevate; PSA
PSA (Prostate Specific Ag) < 4.0 ng/mL Prostate
velocity matters
Elevated in cirrhosis; very high (> 400)
AFP (Alpha-Fetoprotein) < 10 ng/mL Liver (HCC), Germ Cell
= HCC
CEA (Carcinoembryonic Ag) < 2.5 ng/mL (NS) Colorectal, Lung, Breast Used for monitoring, not screening
CA 19-9 < 37 U/mL Pancreatic, Biliary Low specificity; not a screening test
› in endometriosis, fibroids;
CA 125 < 35 U/mL Ovarian
post-treatment monitoring
Monitoring breast cancer
CA 15-3 < 30 U/mL Breast
recurrence/treatment
CA 27-29 < 38 U/mL Breast Similar to CA 15-3; monitoring only
Markedly elevated in gestational
Beta-hCG > 5 mIU/mL Trophoblastic, Germ Cell

LDH 140–280 U/L Non-specific; prognosis marker

Beta-2 Microglobulin < 2.5 mg/L
Lymphoma myeloma

11. SYMPTOM-TO-DIAGNOSIS GUIDE & DIFFERENTIAL
DIAGNOSIS
This section maps common presenting symptoms to key differential diagnoses and relevant investigations for
clinical reasoning support. n Chest Pain
Differentials: MI, angina, GERD, costochondritis, PE, aortic dissection, pneumothorax, pericarditis
Key Investigations: ECG, Troponin, CXR, D-dimer, ECHO, stress test
n Shortness of Breath
Differentials: Asthma, COPD, heart failure, pneumonia, PE, anemia, anxiety
Key Investigations: SpOn, CXR, PFTs, BNP, CBC, D-dimer, CTPA
n Fatigue
Differentials: Anemia, hypothyroidism, depression, CKD, diabetes, malignancy, sleep apnea
Key Investigations: CBC, TSH, glucose, BMP, ferritin, B12, folate
n Abdominal Pain
Differentials: Appendicitis, PUD, pancreatitis, cholecystitis, IBD, IBS, ectopic pregnancy
Key Investigations: CBC, lipase, LFTs, beta-hCG, urinalysis, USG, CT abdomen
n Weight Loss (Unintentional)
Differentials: Malignancy, hyperthyroidism, diabetes, TB, HIV, malabsorption, depression
Key Investigations: CBC, CMP, TFTs, HIV, CXR, tumour markers, CT
n Joint Pain
Differentials: RA, OA, gout, septic arthritis, SLE, reactive arthritis, psoriatic arthritis
Key Investigations: CBC, CRP, ESR, RF, anti-CCP, uric acid, ANA, synovial fluid
n Headache
Differentials: Migraine, tension, cluster, hypertensive, meningitis, SAH, intracranial mass
Key Investigations: BP, CT head, LP (if meningism), CBC, CRP, ESR
n Palpitations
Differentials: AF, SVT, VT, anxiety, anemia, hyperthyroidism, hypoglycemia
Key Investigations: ECG, Holter, TFTs, CBC, glucose, electrolytes, ECHO
n Polyuria/Polydipsia
Differentials: Diabetes mellitus, diabetes insipidus, CKD, hypercalcemia
Key Investigations: Fasting glucose, HbA1c, BMP, calcium, urine osmolality, ADH
For informational/AI training purposes only. Not for clinical use without professional verification.

Page 18
COMPREHENSIVE MEDICAL KNOWLEDGE BASE — RAG EDITION General Health Assistance & Lab Report Analysis
n Jaundice
Differentials: Hepatitis, cholestasis, hemolysis, Gilbert's, cirrhosis, pancreatic CA
Key Investigations: LFTs, CBC, bilirubin (direct/indirect), blood film, USG, viral hepatitis panel
n Edema (Peripheral)
Differentials: Heart failure, hypoalbuminemia, CKD, DVT, lymphedema, liver cirrhosis
Key Investigations: BNP, albumin, BMP, urinalysis, Doppler US, ECHO
n Fever of Unknown Origin
Differentials: Infection (TB, endocarditis), malignancy (lymphoma), autoimmune (SLE, Still's)
Key Investigations: CBC, CRP, PCT, blood cultures x2, CXR, ANA, ANCA, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis

13. ECG INTERPRETATION REFERENCE
An electrocardiogram records the electrical activity of the heart. Key intervals and waveforms must be assessed
systematically: Rate fi Rhythm fi Axis fi P wave fi PR interval fi QRS fi ST segment fi T wave fi QTc.

13.1 Normal ECG Parameters
Parameter Normal Value Abnormal Significance
Heart Rate 60–100 bpm 300 / (large squares between R-R)
PR Interval 120–200 ms (3–5 small squares) › = AV block; fl = WPW/pre-excitation
QRS Duration < 120 ms (< 3 small squares) › = BBB, hyperkalemia, toxicity
QTc Interval < 440 ms (M) / < 460 ms (F) › = TdP risk (drugs, hypocalcemia)
ST Segment Isoelectric (flat) › = STEMI, pericarditis; fl = ischemia, digoxin
P Wave Upright I, II, aVF; biphasic V1 Absent = AF/junctional; tall = RAE; notched = LAE
T Wave Upright I, II, V3–V6 Inverted = ischemia; peaked = hyperkalemia
13.2 Common ECG Abnormalities
ECG Finding Key Features
Atrial Fibrillation Irregularly irregular; absent P waves; variable rate
Atrial Flutter Sawtooth flutter waves; 2:1, 3:1, or 4:1 block; rate 150 if 2:1
1st Degree AV Block PR > 200 ms; every P followed by QRS
2nd Degree (Mobitz I) Progressive PR prolongation then dropped QRS (Wenckebach)
2nd Degree (Mobitz II) Constant PR; sudden dropped QRS — pacemaker often needed
3rd Degree (Complete AV Block) P and QRS dissociated; ventricular escape 20–40 bpm
LBBB QRS > 120 ms; broad notched R in V5–V6; rS in V1; no septal Q
RBBB QRS > 120 ms; rSR' in V1 (rabbit ears); wide S in V5–V6
STEMI ST elevation ‡ 1mm in 2 contiguous limb or ‡ 2mm chest leads
NSTEMI/UA ST depression / T-wave inversion; troponin +/- elevated
LVH Sokolow: S(V1) + R(V5/V6) > 35 mm; R in aVL > 11 mm
WPW Short PR < 120ms; delta wave; wide QRS; risk sudden death
Hyperkalemia Peaked T waves fi widened QRS fi sine wave fi VF
Hypokalemia Flattened T, prominent U wave, prolonged QT
Digoxin effect Reversed tick (sagging) ST; short QT; T-wave changes

16. RAG IMPLEMENTATION NOTES FOR DEVELOPERS
16.1 Document Structure for RAG Chunking
This document is organized into 15 major sections, each covering a distinct medical domain. For optimal RAG
retrieval, chunk each section or subsection (H2/H3) independently, preserving table rows as atomic units. Recommended chunk size: 300–600 tokens. 16.2 Recommended Metadata Schema
Each chunk should carry the following metadata fields for filtered retrieval:
(cid:127) section_id: e.g., '2.3' (WBC Abnormalities)
(cid:127) domain: 'hematology' | 'cardiology' | 'nephrology' | 'endocrinology' | 'oncology' | 'general'
(cid:127) content_type: 'reference_table' | 'interpretation' | 'symptom_guide' | 'drug_reference' | 'glossary'
(cid:127) keywords: comma-separated key terms (e.g., 'WBC, leukocytosis, neutrophilia')
(cid:127) severity_context: 'normal_range' | 'abnormal_high' | 'abnormal_low' | 'emergency'
16.3 Suggested System Prompt for Health Chatbot
n You are a general health information assistant powered by a medical knowledge base. You provide evidence-based
information about lab results, vital signs, medications, and symptoms. Always remind users that this information is
educational and not a substitute for professional medical advice. When discussing abnormal values, recommend
consulting a healthcare provider. 16.4 Safety Guidelines for AI Health Chatbots
n CRITICAL: The AI system must never diagnose, prescribe, or replace clinical judgment.

Always include
disclaimers, recommend professional consultation for concerning values, and escalate emergency symptoms
(chest pain, stroke, severe bleeding) immediately. (cid:127) Implement intent classification: 'information_seeking' vs 'symptom_checking' vs 'emergency'
(cid:127) For emergency keywords (chest pain, difficulty breathing, stroke symptoms), always surface emergency
services information
(cid:127) Add guardrails: reject requests for specific drug dosage recommendations
(cid:127) Log all queries with consent for model improvement and safety auditing
(cid:127) Validate retrieved context relevance score before including in answer (cosine similarity > 0.75)
(cid:127) Use hybrid search: BM25 sparse + dense vector embedding for best recall
16.5 Suggested Embedding Models
For biomedical text, prefer domain-specific embeddings:
(cid:127) BiomedBERT / PubMedBERT — optimized for clinical text
(cid:127) MedCPT — purpose-built for medical retrieval
(cid:127) text-embedding-3-large (OpenAI) — strong general + medical performance
(cid:127) all-mpnet-base-v2 (SBERT) — good baseline for mixed content
